coredata.dc:title: Exploring the association between serum magnesium level and autism spectrum disorder using validated spectrofluorimetric method

coredata.dc:creator: Almalki, Atiah H.
Bamaga, Ahmed K.
Alharbi, Adnan
Abduljabbar, Maram H.
Alnemari, Reem M.
Baali, Fahad H.
Algarni, Majed A.
Ahmed, Marwa F.
Ramzy, Sherif
coredata.dc:description: Magnesium is an essential mineral in biological systems and has a significant impact on brain health. Its deficiency has been found to correlate with irregular metabolic processes and neurodevelopmental disorders. The objective of this research was to establish and validate an analytical approach based on the standard addition methodology for determining endogenous magnesium levels in the serum of autistic and healthy children. Analytically, the approach involved functionalizing fluorescent graphene quantum dots with a magnesium-phosphomolybdic acid ion pair complex, followed by measuring magnesium-induced fluorescence quenching on the functionalized graphene quantum dots, which is concentration-dependent. The approach was validated in accordance with the ICH M10 requirements for bioanalytical technique validation, and it reliably quantified magnesium concentrations in the serum of both autistic and healthy children. The study found that autistic children have considerably lower serum magnesium concentrations than healthy children (P < 0.01), indicating a correlation between magnesium deficiency and autism spectrum disorder. The average serum magnesium levels (mg/dl) recorded for the autistic and healthy groups were 2.03 ± 0.33 and 2.28 ± 0.26, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Autism spectrum disorder
Serum magnesium level
Standard addition method
Functionalized graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of the F-GQDs.
Fluorescence quenching of F-GQDs in correlation with rising magnesium concentrations.
Effect of the pH on the Fluorescence quenching using 10 μg/mL magnesium.
Effect of the F-GQDs concentration on the Fluorescence quenching using 10 μg/mL magnesium.
Effect of the incubation time on the Fluorescence quenching using 10 μg/mL magnesium.
Example of SAM calibration curve for magnesium determination in real serum sample (dilution factor, 1:10 v/v).
The outcomes of accuracy and precision.
Mean (± standard deviation) serum magnesium levels in autistic (n = 24) and healthy (n = 15) groups.

head.ce:title: Exploring the association between serum magnesium level and autism spectrum disorder using validated spectrofluorimetric method

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnesium is an essential mineral in biological systems and has a significant impact on brain health. Its deficiency has been found to correlate with irregular metabolic processes and neurodevelopmental disorders. The objective of this research was to establish and validate an analytical approach based on the standard addition methodology for determining endogenous magnesium levels in the serum of autistic and healthy children. Analytically, the approach involved functionalizing fluorescent graphene quantum dots with a magnesium-phosphomolybdic acid ion pair complex, followed by measuring magnesium-induced fluorescence quenching on the functionalized graphene quantum dots, which is concentration-dependent. The approach was validated in accordance with the ICH M10 requirements for bioanalytical technique validation, and it reliably quantified magnesium concentrations in the serum of both autistic and healthy children. The study found that autistic children have considerably lower serum magnesium concentrations than healthy children (P < 0.01), indicating a correlation between magnesium deficiency and autism spectrum disorder. The average serum magnesium levels (mg/dl) recorded for the autistic and healthy groups were 2.03 ± 0.33 and 2.28 ± 0.26, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Micronutrient deficiencies can precipitate a range of neurodevelopmental disorders, including autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) [1]. Notably, a significant proportion of children diagnosed with ASD frequently present with inflammatory bowel disease and gastrointestinal inflammation, thereby heightening their susceptibility to micronutrient deficiencies [2]. A number of scholarly investigations have established a correlation between ASD and deficiencies in essential micronutrients [3–10].

Magnesium (Mg) is an essential micronutrient that is involved in more than 300 biochemical enzymatic activities, including DNA synthesis, nerve and muscle functions, bone formation, and energy production. Magnesium plays a significant role in brain health [11]. It plays a critical role in neurotransmitter activity regulation, modulates nerve impulse conduction, and serves as a protective agent against excitotoxicity, a phenomenon that has the potential to cause cellular damage [12]. Consequently, insufficient magnesium levels can significantly affect both physical and mental health.

The establishment of validated bioanalytical methods for measuring micronutrient levels in biological fluids is crucial for understanding the correlation between micronutrients’ status and diseases [13]. Nonetheless, analysts face challenges in developing a validated bioanalytical procedure using conventional matrix-matched calibration techniques, especially if there is significant variability in endogenous micronutrient concentrations in the studied participants and there is no serum matrix free of endogenous micronutrients. Recently, the ICH guideline M10 on the validation of bioanalytical techniques defined four different strategies for the quantification of endogenous analyte concentrations, thereby addressing these intrinsic limitations. These strategies encompassed the standard addition method (SAM), the background subtraction method, the surrogate matrix method, and the surrogate analyte method [14].

The objective of the current study was to establish a validated bioanalytical spectrofluorimetric methodology that employed the SAM strategy for quantifying the levels of endogenous magnesium in the serum of both autistic and healthy children. The spectrofluorimetric assay consists of two distinct phases: the functionalization of the surface of authenticated fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a magnesium-phosphomolybdic acid (Mg-PMA) ion pair complex, followed by the measurement of the quenching characters of magnesium on the functionalized GQDs fluorescence, which is concentration-dependent. The developed method demonstrated a negligible matrix effect and was effectively utilized for the quantification of endogenous magnesium concentrations in the serum of both autistic and healthy children. The study found that autistic children have considerably lower serum magnesium concentrations than healthy children, indicating a correlation between magnesium deficiency and ASD.
FP-6200 spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan), 3510 benchtop pH meter (Jenway, UK), digital analytical balance (Precisa, Switzerland).

Phosphomolybdic acid (PMA) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The analytical grade reagents, including magnesium chloride, boric acid, potassium chloride, sodium hydroxide, potassium phosphate, acetic acid, sodium acetate, hydrochloric acid, and potassium biphthalate, were obtained from ADWIC (Egypt). Furthermore, ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, and chloroform were procured from Merck KGaA (Germany).

A stock solution of magnesium chloride at a concentration of 2000 μg/mL was made by dissolving 0.2 g of magnesium chloride in distilled water in a 100-mL volumetric flask. Three working solutions were prepared by further dilution of stock solution with distilled water at final concentrations of 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, and 200 μg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of magnesium-phosphomolybdic acid (Mg-PMA) ion pair complex
ce:section.ce:para: The Mg-PMA ion pair complex was prepared by mixing 50 mL Mg (1.0 × 10−2 M) and 50 mL PMA (1.0 × 10−2 M). The resultant precipitate underwent filtration, was thoroughly washed with distilled water, and subsequently air-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of GQDs with Mg-PMA ion pair complex

ce:section.ce:para: This study was approved by the Ethical Committee of the Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Damietta, Egypt (approval code: DFM-IRB 000122367-24-11-007). All procedures adhered to the principles outlined in the Declaration of Helsinki. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants before their enrollment in the trial. Blood specimens were obtained from pediatric participants involved in this research at Al-Azhar University Hospital located in New Damietta, Egypt, subsequent to the procurement of written informed consent from their parents. Two cohorts of children, matched by age (ranging from 4 to 12 years), were recruited for the study. The first cohort consists of 24 diagnosed autistic children, whereas the second cohort is composed of 15 healthy children. Blood specimens were allowed to coagulate before centrifugation. The resulting serum was collected and stored at −20 °C for later analysis.

In the SAM technique, a separate and specific calibration curve was constructed for each serum specimen. Every serum specimen was divided into four aliquots, each with a volume of 1 mL, in a set of centrifuge tubes. Three aliquots were individually spiked with 1 mL of the working solutions and were referred to as “SAM-spiked-samples” while the fourth aliquot remained non-spiked and was referred to as “SAM-non-spiked-sample”. Each sample underwent treatment with 4 mL of acetonitrile, subjected to vortex agitation for 1 min, followed by centrifugation for 30 min. The resultant supernatants were subsequently evaporated to achieve complete dryness. The resulting residues were reconstituted in a suitable volume of water, transferred into a set of 10-mL volumetric flasks. Each flask received 0.5 mL of the F-GQDs solution and 0.75 mL of alkaline borate buffer solution pH 9, and the flasks were diluted to the mark with water. The flasks were lefts to a 5-min incubation period, after which the fluorescence intensity of each sample (F) was quantified at a wavelength of 445 nm subsequent to excitation at 352 nm. The fluorescence difference (
Δ F =
F 0
− F
) between F-GQDs (F
0 ) and samples (F) was determined. The SAM calibration curve for each serum specimen was generated by graphing the ΔF values in relation to the concentrations of the samples [SAM-non-spiked-sample (0 μg/mL), and SAM-spiked-samples (5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL)]. Subsequently, the endogenous magnesium concentration for each serum specimen was ascertained as the negative x-intercept of the SAM calibration curve formulated for that specific specimen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectra characteristics and optimization of experimental parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of the fluorescent GQDs was functionalized through the incorporation of a Mg-PMA ion pair complex to enhance its selectivity for magnesium detection. The F-GQDs exhibited a unique fluorescence emission signal at 445 nm subsequent to excitation at 352 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The fluorescence intensity of the F-GQDs was markedly diminished upon interaction with magnesium ions. The quenching properties exhibited a progressive increase in correlation with rising magnesium concentrations, as demonstrated in Fig. 2 .

The experimental parameters that affected the quenching efficiency of F-GQDs were tested and optimized. The influence of pH on quenching efficiency was investigated across a broad spectrum ranging from 3 to 12, revealing that quenching efficiency increased with increasing pH, with the best results obtained using a borate buffer at pH 9, as shown in Fig. 3 . The quenching efficiency was further tested at various amounts of borate buffer pH 9, with the most favorable results obtained by using 0.75 mL of the buffer solution. Given that the concentration of F-GQDs may influence the quenching efficiency, a range of F-GQDs quantities was investigated, with the optimal outcomes achieved by the using of 0.5 mL of the F-GQDs solution, as depicted in Fig. 4 . Furthermore, different diluting solvents were tested for their effect on the quenching efficiency of F-GQDs, with water providing the best results. The fluorescence quenching kinetics of F-GQDs was also examined at different time intervals between 0 and 20 min. The results indicate that the highest quenching occurred within 5 min, indicating that magnesium interacted quickly with F-GQDs surface (Fig. 5 ).

The methodology was validated in accordance with the guidelines outlined in the ICH M10 draft for the quantification of endogenous compounds [14], in conjunction with the applications described by Coglianese et al. [15].

The SAM calibration curve (see Fig. 6 ) for each serum specimen was generated by graphing the ΔF values in relation to the concentrations of the samples [SAM-non-spiked-sample (0 μg/mL), and SAM-spiked-samples (5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL)]. The equation of SAM calibration curve is expressed as y = ax + b, where (a) denotes the slope and (b) represents the intercept. The intercept (b) value signifies the ΔF value calculated for the SAM-non-spiked-sample, which is greater than zero due to the presence of an unknown endogenous amount of magnesium in the real sample. By setting y = 0, the endogenous magnesium concentration in the SAM-non-spiked-sample (x) could be calculated as the negative x-intercept on the calibration curve using a specific equation:
(1)
x = −
b a

From equation (1), the endogenous magnesium concentrations in the SAM-spiked-samples (x
n ) were also estimated. This could be achieved by calculating the intercept values of each SAM-spiked-sample (b
n ) as follow:
(2)
b n
=
(
a ∗
x
+ n
)
−
y n
Where (x + n ) denotes the theoretical concentration added to each SAM-spiked-sample (5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL), and (y
n ) signifies the ΔF value determined for each SAM-spiked-sample in the calibration curve. The estimated b
n values were used to compute the endogenous magnesium concentration (x
n ) in each SAM-spiked-sample using equation (1). The endogenous magnesium concentration in every serum specimen was determined as the average of the endogenous concentrations estimated in SAM-non-spiked-sample (x) and SAM-spiked-samples (x
n ), as illustrated in Fig. 6.

Accuracy and precision were evaluated by measuring the concentration added to each SAM-spiked-sample of the calibration curve (c + n). A single SAM calibration curve was evaluated three times throughout one day for intra-day calculations, and three times over three days for inter-day calculations. The endogenous magnesium concentration (x
n ) in each SAM-spiked-sample was calculated using equation (1). The magnesium concentration added to each SAM-spiked-sample was then estimated using the equation below:
(3)
c
+ n
=
(
y n
a
)
−
x n

The %recovery (%R) at each SAM-spiked-sample, which indicates the percent ratio of experimental and theoretical magnesium concentrations added to a specific sample, was computed as follows:
(4)
%
R
+ n
=
(
c
+ n
x
+ n
)
∗ 100

For each SAM-spiked-sample, the mean %R and percent coefficient of variation (%CV) were determined to assess accuracy and precision. Accepted accuracy is ±15 % of the theoretical magnesium concentration, while accepted precision (%CV) is less than 15 %. Table 1 displays the computed accuracy and precision values for each SAM-spiked-sample, all of which are within the established acceptable limits.

The assessment of the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) in the absence of serum samples devoid of endogenous magnesium poses significant challenges. Consequently, we conducted an evaluation of the instrumental LLOQ (iLLOQ) utilizing water as a neat blank matrix, alongside the procedure LLOQ (pLLOQ) in real serum matrix. In iLLOQ assessment, samples at a concentration of 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, and 1 μg/mL were prepared in water and analyzed in triplicate, with subsequent calculation of the ΔF values. The iLLOQ is regarded as the lowest magnesium concentration that provides a ΔF value larger than 20, which was reached in this work with 0.3 μg/mL. In pLLOQ assessment, a pooled serum sample with low magnesium levels was aliquoted into five 1 mL portions. Four samples were spiked with magnesium at concentrations of 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 μg/mL, while one sample remained non-spiked. The samples underwent triplicate analysis, leading to the formulation of the SAM calibration curve. The accuracy and precision of each spiked sample were evaluated. The pLLOQ is regarded as the minimal analyte concentration in serum that can be detected with acceptable accuracy (%R ±15 %) and precision (%CV ≤ 15 %). The accuracy and precision metrics in Table 2 demonstrated that 1 μg/mL was the lowest added concentration that yielded satisfactory accuracy and precision results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of the endogenous magnesium levels is autistic and healthy children
ce:section.ce:para: The endogenous magnesium concentrations in the serum of the children participating in this study, 24 autistic and 15 healthy, was determined using the established method. Table 3 reveals that autistic children have considerably lower serum magnesium concentrations compared to healthy children (P < 0.01). The average serum magnesium levels (mg/dl) for the autistic and healthy cohorts were recorded as 2.03 ± 0.33 and 2.28 ± 0.26, respectively.

In conclusion, a validated bioanalytical spectrofluorimetric methodology that employed the SAM strategy for quantifying the levels of endogenous magnesium in the serum of both autistic and healthy children was developed. The spectrofluorimetric assay is based on the measurement of the quenching effect of magnesium on the fluorescent F-GQDs, which exhibits a concentration-dependent relationship. The method developed exhibited minimal matrix effects, as the same biological matrix and analyte were utilized for both study samples and calibration standards. The implemented methodology was proficiently applied for the quantification of endogenous magnesium levels in the serum of both autistic and healthy children, with findings suggesting that autistic children exhibit significantly lower serum magnesium concentrations when contrasted with their healthy counterparts.

Atiah H. Almalki: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ahmed K. Bamaga: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Adnan Alharbi: Investigation, Data curation. Maram H. Abduljabbar: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Reem M. Alnemari: Formal analysis, Investigation. Fahad H. Baali: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Majed A. Algarni: Formal analysis, Validation. Marwa F. Ahmed: Writing – review & editing. Sherif Ramzy: Writing – original draft, Methodology.
This research was funded by Taif University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (TU-DSPP-2024-175).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relations that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia, for supporting this work through project number (TU-DSPP-2024-175).
